items.q: What is produced in anaerobic respiration in human muscle cells?
items.correct: Lactic acid (lactate) and a small amount of ATP — no CO2 or water in this pathway.
items.misconception: Anaerobic respiration produces ethanol and CO2 in humans.
items.random_wrong: Anaerobic respiration produces oxygen as a waste product.
items.why: Students confuse anaerobic respiration in yeast (alcohol + CO2) with the human form (lactic acid).

items.q: What does it mean for an allele to be 'dominant'?
items.correct: It is expressed in the phenotype when at least one copy is present (in a heterozygote).
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common allele in a population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele physically destroys the recessive allele in the genome.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'dominant' (most prevalent, strongest) misleads students into thinking it relates to frequency or power.

items.q: What makes the antigen-binding site of an antibody specific to one antigen?
items.correct: The variable region — its amino acid sequence (and thus shape) differs between antibodies, complementing a specific antigen.
items.misconception: All antibodies have the same binding site; specificity comes from where they go in the body.
items.random_wrong: Antibodies bind to any antigen they meet first.
items.why: Students miss the role of the variable region in determining specificity, thinking antibodies are interchangeable generic 'germ-killers'.

items.q: Which bond forms between two amino acids?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peptide. “Glycosidic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Haemoglobin has 4 subunits — this is which level?
items.why: It's Quaternary. “Primary” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A polypeptide has 80 amino acids. How many peptide bonds does it contain?
items.why: The real figure is 79. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
